I computed the total return based on two methods:

  1. Average duration: We have Total Profit / Total Cost = $0.44/$13.19 = 3.33%. Then, weighted by the average position duration: 3.33% * 208 total days / 104 days = 6.66% for a total of 208 days.
  2. Average cost: Time-weighted average Cost = $6.65. Then, Total profit/Average cost = $0.44/$6.65 = 6.66%, the same.

If we compare with the stock strictly for those 208 days, with Microsoft at ~$495 on Jun-27, the stock outperformed, with a 17% return. However, if we compare them until today, the Put Spread was better, bringing a profit compared to a loss of ~5% for the stock.

Regardless of that, I was not very satisfied with that return; I usually target much more with this strategy. Now, part of that is because Microsoft is inherently a less volatile stock (therefore, with lower uncertainty), and less IV (Implied Volatility) brings lower option premiums (including for spreads). We can’t compare a bull Put Spread in Microsoft with a bull Put Spread in Coherent (COHR), which brought me 61.2% in about 6 months, because Coherent has a much higher IV but also a higher uncertainty.

But part of that is because of a combination of not-great timing (I started with a short 395/385 Put, although I would have preferred lower) and over-conservatism (for the 350/340 Put, I should have increased my exposure with the stock undervalued, but I didn’t), and I think I am much better positioned today. With Microsoft close to $400 these days, I re-initiated a bull Put Spread strategy with a bit more interesting premiums, helped by a high IV, due to this strange pressure on all software-related stocks (but if all software ETFs are under pressure, of course that Microsoft will feel it too):

  • Short 360 Put Nov’26.
  • Long 355 Put Nov’26.

On average, I got $1.58 credit these days for this position until now, my target being between 2/3 and ¾ of a normal position size. Let’s see how it performs at expiration but also if closed early:

With a maximum return of ~46%, that beats a stock position until ~$587, which I consider with low probability until November. But what is most important is that it can do that without big movements in the stock price. Let’s see some possible scenarios:

Scenario 1: The stock has a big rally towards its Fair Value, both Puts will have very low extrinsic value, being far OTM (Out-Of-The-Money), I can close the position for close to its maximum return, perhaps ~45%. Then, with Microsoft fairly valued, I will very likely pass.

Scenario 2: The stock will languish in this interval (+/- 10%). I will likely close it for 40%+ close to the expiration date, when extrinsic value is low. With low IV, that could be about one month before expiration (somewhere in October). With high IV, that could be about one week before expiration. But now, with Microsoft still undervalued, I could just roll over my strategy for another target of ~45% per nine months. Overall, an annualized return could be around 60%.

Scenario 3: The stock is heading down. I will close the position as soon as possible with the stock moving from $360 to $355. With the stock at $350 at the end of August, the loss would be ~33% (estimated, depending on the IV at that time), but with the stock at $350 at expiration, the loss would be 100%, because both Puts will be ITM (In-The-Money), with zero extrinsic value. There is also the risk here that the stock could suddenly jump from ~$370 to ~$355, and that can happen especially after just released earnings; that’s why I will watch the position, especially before earnings; maybe I will roll it over with the stock at ~$370 just 2-3 days before earnings. Now, I will roll down and over my position (targeting profitability above ~$320-$325), but this time being more careful to add more funds (perhaps a normal-sized position this time), so that a potential ~45% return will be magnified compared to the ~33% loss for ~2/3 of the position.

Takeaway

Microsoft's economic moat remains strong across ~90% of its business, with quasi-monopolies in Office and Windows, multiple moat sources for Azure, high switching costs for dynamics, and a narrow moat in gaming and ads.

Derived from a DCF model and backed by a comparison with 5-year averages, my fair value estimate is $545, driven by double-digit revenue growth and increasing operating margin due to higher scale and lower expenses brought by its Maia chip.

Key risks include high CapEx, especially for short-lived assets, and significant reliance on OpenAI, which now accounts for 45% of commercial RPO. I increased my WACC to accommodate these risks, but especially OpenAI's balance between growth and profitability is to be followed.

I favor a bull Put Spread strategy, with up to ~45% return, which I consider well suited to Microsoft's lower volatility and capped upside, and with defined downside, outperforming the stock in stagnant, modestly rising, or even slightly decreasing scenarios.

data.title: Corsair Gaming: Reporting Day Gains Show Undervaluation For 2026E
data.description:
It needs to be said that my position in Corsair (CRSR) is not significant at this time. I bought after citing "BUY" in my last article from about 4-5 months ago, which you can find here. I further added that when the company fell down in November/December, my position was at about 0.25% of my portfolio. The reason that I kept my position small was a combination of the risks I cited in my article, as well as my overall reticence to invest in this market, which I believe to be overvalued.

The volatility of the market impacts especially companies that in themselves are very volatile. This couldn't be presented better than with the results we have from Corsair today. I would argue that the non-GAAP beat of 16 cents and a slight revenue beat (about $15M) were not significant enough to change any part of my long-term thesis for the company, yet the company's share price reacted as though something miraculous had happened and shot up 49-50% at the time of writing this article.

This sort of reaction shows me two things.

First, it shows me the volatility that's being included in the stock. When a company goes up like this on news that we had today, it can be related to demand/supply imbalances, momentum trading, or speculation. It can also indicate institutional buying - the big guys moving in. If you're already established, like I am, as an owner, then that's a good thing. It means your position is going up, simply because some investors may now be expecting more positive news. Generally speaking, though, I'm always very careful about sudden movements in any stock, as it implies the stock is hard to forecast.

Secondly, it could also be a short squeeze - but I'm not sure that's the case here, with a relatively limited amount of Corsair shares being shorted relative to what significant shorted stocks see (5.5% is not zero, but it's also not the highest).

In this article, I'll cite the reasons and the potential for the company, relative to my previous thesis on the company, and show you why I believe the company may outperform further.

Why, in essence, I am sticking to my targets. It's a good lesson for those of you not familiar with my approach or my thinking. The fact that I don't grasp my targets out of the aether, but there's significant consideration and calculation behind them, so it takes a long time to change them.

Let's see what we have here.

Corsair - Update for 2026 shows us significant upside, buybacks, and potential

Let me start straight off by saying that I'm very surprised that a memory player (as in DDR) like Corsair stayed down for so long. I've been seeing for some months a persistent weakness in the stock, not meshing together well with the expected and realized surge in memory pricing. They don't manufacture memory, which is why it had a relatively small profile in my previous article, but the company is an integrator of DRAM. They buy circuits and design them for customers. They work in the higher-end segments of DRAM. This also means that it's not a direct beneficiary of memory upside, as some might have you believe, but instead a secondary beneficiary as a component engineer (as opposed to a manufacturer).

Still, the upside is definitely there, with memory prices rising.

Aside from this, Corsair also saw several interesting other trends – but these, I forecasted more clearly in my last piece.

The company reported double-digit revenue and a 30% gross profit increase. The EBITDA increase was within my range of guidance because I expected higher than the company forecasted for (again, due to some memory strength). This was for the full year – but the Q4 results on a YoY basis were excellent. Memory strength was on display here, with it being the reason for segment revenue growth. Whenever you see system interest growth and gaming component growth – well, you can't build a computer without some type of DRAM.

The company characterizes it as superb execution – and I'm sure that played a part in it as well, but I would more relegate the company's success to market backdrop factors. I suspected and expected the company to enact SCM improvements to handle the tariffs, and the company's roadmap of products and services remains, as I said in my previous article, exciting.

The company's southward journey over the past few months has not in any way reflected the company's AEPS growth or estimates. That is why I stayed positive and also why I bought more. Despite very low debt, good sales, and a good market cap, and despite consistent growth estimates over 25% per year on average, the company has not been appreciated by the market – at least it has not until now.

What I see now is the company more closely tracking to a non-cheap valuation. If the company had reported poor results for the quarter and year, and we had instead seen a drop, I would now have cited the company as being "cheap" based on my PT of $9.5/share. I remind you that before market open today, the company traded below $4.6/share, which would have been considered cheap, based on my typical expectation of 30-40% below the PT. For a volatile company like this one, I'd lower that further, but even in that case, it would have fulfilled that.

Corsair's forward strategy is dependent on what we'll call integration of ecosystems, things that it makes. It needs to further strengthen its position in niches, and this in turn is dependent on walking a precarious balancing act between cost efficiency and delivering high-value and premium products. There's certainly some surge of what we've seen that's related to expected AI upside as well, though at this time, I'm uncertain as to what portion this can be said to be dependent on this.

The news is, to me, nothing new. Corsair is basically reiterating the growth strategy from my previous article. Invest in platforms that scale, the Stream Deck, new product launches (the Galleon Keyboard, a great success), and generally continue what they are doing with their new mix strategy - the reason that I invested in the company in the first place.

As such, the targets or the strategy haven't changed. I'm guessing the $50M stock buyback plans have something to do with the share price performance as well.

To say that Corsair has now, because of these results, become a "safe investment" or a good stock would be going too far. To say that we can take this as a bit of a confirmation that the strategy is working, I'd say that's not far from the truth.

Whenever you invest in a business like this, ups and downs are to be expected. The biggest mistake that many investors make is that they let themselves (their decision-making in terms of buying and selling) be driven by what the market irrationally believes in the short term. While there is a time, certainly, to act to market reactions, the decision usually isn't to sell because of a massive crash. In my entire investment career, I can count on two fingers the time when I have sold a stake because the thesis, the fundamentals, and the company itself were in danger of falling apart.

And if the decline (which we've seen in Corsair over the past 3 months) is not driven by fundamentals or actual appeal (or lack of it), then it's driven by sentiment.

And sentiment isn't enough to make me change my long-term thesis.

I'm now up in my investment in Corsair. If I had bought at the very bottom, one could make an argument for selling into a 50% gain in a short time. But I'm up about 15%, and at this time, I don't believe a 21% gain in a 0.25% position (despite that being non-trivial in the cash amount) is simply not worth it.

Thus, I move to valuation and state the following targets and assumptions.

Valuation for Corsair - Mostly unchanged, fair value up by about 5%

I spent the better parts of the last few hours updating my assumptions and modeling for this company - and what I have found that would cause me to change or adjust my outlook is very little. What we have is a projected increase in DRAM pricing, which I will use to argue for a higher fair value potential, but not PT. Despite the general trend regarding gaming peripherals having grown more bullish since my last article, and one could argue for using that for estimating a higher share price, I will not do that at this time.

As a conservative investor, and this being a volatile company (case in point, today's market move), I maintain my $9.5/share PT (remember, 2 articles ago it was at $8/share; if it had gone down, it might have necessitated an adjustment such as that), but I believe that the fair value is now up to $15/share, or a 20x forward P/E for 2027E assuming a $0.7-$0.8/share AEPS range. This in turn, even after today, would imply a 15% annualized return.

That being said, the peripheral market continues to be volatile at best, and forecasting it with anything assuming a high grade of accuracy would be a touch arrogant, and ignoring the context of what the company does, as well as the accuracy the company brings to the table.

Or, more accurately, the lack of any such accuracy in the forecast.

I remain a very conservative, patient, and value-driven investor. I see value in Corsair Gaming here, albeit in the short term. I fully believe this company is capable of a $15/share fair value, and I definitely believe in $9-10/share, which is my current PT.

Because the 4Q25 results have only reiterated and confirmed my expectations, I give this company a rating of "BUY", with an updated fair value target.

Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Several risks exist for Corsair, despite superb results. The company faces competition from other established brands in the gaming space, like Asus, Acer, and Logitech (LOGI), which I also invest in. Corsair's debt situation, despite improving due to better earnings, also requires discounting. And I want to reiterate very clearly that this is a speculative "BUY" - there are many safer companies out there, and many others that I invest in before this one.

Beyond that, this company is a "BUY" with a reiterated PT of $9.5/share.

Thesis

Corsair is a market-leading sort of gaming peripheral and component business with an eye towards a market upswing when the cyclical market reverts due to the release of a new generation of products, which are likely to be released, in this analyst's opinion, somewhere in 2026-2028. As of Q4 2025, I believe this general trend has been confirmed, at least for the initial part of 2026.

You still need to be okay with sub-IG rating levels; you need to be okay with no dividend (for the time being, though I still think that this may change), and you need to be okay with this company not having a long time in the market (I have now been invested for about 6 months on average, lower for most of my position because I bought more in November and December). If you're okay with all of this and have a period of 3-4 years, then I see a potential upside of 20-30% per year, which meets my dividend-less RoR potential for an investment.

For that reason, I say "Buy" with a PT of around $9.5/share. I update my fair value to $15/share here, as of 4Q25.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a valuation target of $9.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of February 2026. It should be noted that this is somewhat speculative, however.

data.title: Oracle: A Look At The 6.6% Yielding Preferred Shares
data.description:
The Oracle Corporation (ORCL) story has been an exciting one to follow. The company has been one of the rare tech stocks to make a 52-week high and then saw a 60% drawdown in the last six months.

We have held a skeptical view of this experiment of building billions of data centers for "inference AI demand."

Last time around, we looked at the 7% yielding bonds for the company. This time, we go over the recently issued preferred shares.

Oracle Corporation 6.5 DEP CUM SR D (ORCL.PR.D) Terms

The 6.6% yield may look appealing from ORCL, but things are not what they seem. The stock is a mandatory convertible.

The preferred shares are mandatorily convertible on 01/15/2029 into a variable number of Oracle Corp common shares based on the then current price of the common shares for 20 consecutive trading days immediately prior to the conversion date.

Source: Quantum Online

In case this is the first time you have come across it, we can explain. These shares are pseudo common equity. They function and move around like common equity with a small quirk that they pay very large dividends. We had first spoken about these kinds of securities when we wrote about Algonquin Power & Utilities Corp. (AQN). Back then, a large bunch of investors who were unfamiliar with mandatory convertibles ran right over the cliff thinking they were going to collect 8% returns with virtually no risk. Since then, though, we have seen plenty of these mandatory convertibles come up, and the public should be aware of what they represent. ORCL's pricing sheet below also clarifies that there is no real alpha here outside of a temporary arbitrage, which might creep in from time to time.

The conversion settlement rate will be 0.2499 shares per Depositary Share if the then current market price is equal to or greater than $200.07 and 0.3124 shares per Depositary Share if the market price is equal to or less than $160.06. For market prices between those values the settlement rate will be $50 divided by the market value.

Source: Quantum Online

If you multiply $200.07 by 0.2499, you get $50.00. The same is true with $160.06 into $0.3124. For prices between these, the language again tells you that you land up with $50.00 worth of common shares.

Of course there is the dividend.

Cumulative distributions of 6.50% per annum ($3.25 per annum or $0.8125 per quarter) will be paid quarterly on 1/15, 4/15, 7/15 & 11/15 to holders of record on 1/1, 4/1, 7/1 & 11/1 prior to the payment date (NOTE: the ex-dividend date is the record date).

Source: Quantum Online

ORCL common shares yield just 1.28%, so this is a big improvement. But there is a catch. The catch is that everyone knows about this, and the price should reflect this. Let's see the current price to run through the calculations and see whether this is true.

At $158.62, your preferred shares are worth $158.62 X 0.3124 = $49.55. This appears to be fairly cheap. You might think you are getting an ORCL-linked price plus a 5%-plus yield differential between preferred and common. But in reality, if ORCL price goes to $200 by the time of conversion, the shares you get on conversion, drops, and you essentially are getting just $50.00. So that high yield is offset by the lack of capital appreciation. There is a scenario where you might want to own these, though. If you believe that ORCL goes to the moon, then yes, you could get paid 6.6% and then get huge capital appreciation as well. As you might have noticed, over $200.07, you don't get less shares. So if the price of ORCL common goes to $400.14, your preferreds become worth $100.00. You can see this below in the value of $100,000 invested in ORCL preferred shares. (SEC Filing Link)

Valuation & Key Risks

The key risk for running after this security is that ORCL is now grappling with the largest capex cycle in its history. Its liabilities and off-balance-sheet commitments are problems if there are any missteps. In our opinion, we don't see any way this works out.

Taking a mandatory convertible is a real bad way to play this.

Verdict

The issuance of mandatory convertibles is a silly trend that we really hope would stop. We are unsure why they are being done. Perhaps the feeling is that companies that bought back their own stock at atrocious prices might be feeling that issuing common equity looks bad when the stock has fallen. But in any case, there is no real justification for the investor pursuing these. If you are interested in income, just do longer dated covered calls.

You can produce more than 22% annual returns with that covered call. That's right. If ORCL stays flat over the next two years, you make about $49 per share on $109 of deployed capital ($159 stock price minus $49 covered call cash received). You also get the dividends from the stock, and that all works out to 22% plus annually. That looks a little (ok, a lot) better than the 6.6% on the preferred, which can give you all the jostling of the common shares. At present, ORCL is a bit oversold, and we wouldn't wager short bets at this point. We continue to rate it a "hold" with the caveat that the company looks uninvestable. The preferred shares do fall in that category as well. Sophisticated investors might be able to figure out complex trades where you buy the preferred and sell calls on the common shares. But that is beyond the scope of this article.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it or sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Economic Data, Earnings Updates
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Jobs data and other macro factors (0:25) Economic picture holding steady (3:00) AI expansions, tech earnings (5:25) Fed update, Walmart valuation, earnings coming (10:30)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: It's Friday the 13th, it's Wall Street Roundup. Welcome back, Brian Stewart, Seeking Alpha's Director of News.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here.

Rena Sherbill: Always great to have you talk to us. What are you looking at this week? What are you thinking about?

Brian Stewart: So I think this week was more about macro factors. I'm thinking especially about the jobs data, the inflation data that just came out today.

So looking at the jobs data, added 130,000 jobs in January. Unemployment rate dipped to 4.3%. Those are both better than expected. Unfortunately, we saw revisions lower in previous months.

December went down 2000 from 50 to 48,000 and November was down to 41,000 job gain. So pretty, pretty modest there. Also, we saw a downward revision for the 2025 as a whole. And that was pretty large revision lower. So previously the total number of jobs added in 2025 was slightly less than 600,000. So 584,000. And that was revised down to 181,000. So something like a third of the job gains that we thought we had.

You saw revisions lower in each of the months except for one. 2025, we had losses in four of the 12 months with the new revised data. So as it turns out, the situation in 2025 was pretty anemic in terms of job growth.

I think people are balancing the better than expected news for January with the realization that 2025 was probably not as good as we expected. And even, in the moment, didn't feel that great.

And I also think there's a bit of skepticism about the January report just because there has been this habit of revising lower in the future. So we might see that number come down in coming months. So, we'll have to see it's still a pretty other jobs data has been pointing to weakness. We saw the higher layoffs, we saw the lower job openings.

So all in all, seems like a pretty rough job market for people out there right now.

Meanwhile, looking at inflation, CPI came in cooler than expected 2.4 % for the headline number, 2.5 % for core CPI. It's not what you want when the core is higher than headline. Also, even though the headline number was better than expected, it's still above the 2% target for the Fed.

We're still in that same mini stagflation situation where job growth is shaky and we have a situation where inflation continues to run a little.

Rena Sherbill: Anything else you would add to the broader economic picture? I know we saw some housing data also. Anything else to add to fill out the picture?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, I think we're getting a few housing companies reporting earnings next week, too. So we're going to get some more data on that front. think overall, if you just take the economic picture as a whole, I think it's holding steady in this kind of unsure area where we and if we take this in market terms.

So we've we've had the AI trade for a while. We've also seen AI take over more and more of the capital expenditures of these companies. So in that sense, it's becoming a larger part of the economy.

But the question becomes, how's the rest of the economy doing? So I think it gives just concerns about whether or not the AI situation is going to be enough of a pillar to kind of prop up the economy going forward.

Just a couple of specific stocks that play into that. So Caterpillar (CAT) reported earnings in late January. It was only a modest increase on its earnings report, it rose 3%. So people kind of shrugged when that came out.

But ever since then, it's continued to move higher. And a lot of this is on the capital expenditure announcements by a lot of the larger hyperscalers. The main driver of growth at Caterpillar has been its power systems unit. So basically building the power generating capacity for these hyperscalers to run the data centers that they're looking to build.

Since the earnings have come out, there's been just sort of this melt up from Caterpillar, which is now up 21 % since those earnings came out. It's at a new high. It's risen 37 % year to date. It's up 121 % in the past year.

So you see how the money being spent by the Amazons. (AMZN), the Googles (GOOG) (GOOGL), the Metas (META) of the world, how that money kind of trickles down to these other companies. So I think there's a lot of work being done among investors trying to figure out who the specific beneficiaries are going to be for this, this outpouring of cash that's coming into.

Rena Sherbill: What else stock wise are you looking at this week?

Brian Stewart: Well, I think on that same topic, it's interesting to note that Alphabet is selling debt to pay for these things. So we talked about how the amount of money that that's been sort of earmarked for these AI expansions, outstrips the amount of profit that these companies are making, talking about like alphabet and meta. and so that money's gotta come from somewhere, right?

So Alphabet is selling debt. They sold a hundred year bond. so they're really pushing the idea of AI into the pretty far future in terms of paying for it.

Meanwhile, you did have some tech names announcing earnings this week, kind of a split decision. Cisco (CSCO) fell 12%, earnings guidance topped expectations, but there were concerns about margin and costs there. The stock is coming off a high, so even with the decline of 12 % after its earnings report, it's still up 19 % on a one-year basis, and it's only down slightly year-to-date.

So basically, that's giving back some of the gains that had gone before. you see, these companies are getting to the point where they really, really have to impress to get another tick higher.

Valuation concerns are really coming to a fore. However, on the other side of that, you have (AMAT), Applied Materials up 10 % on earnings, reach new highs with that pop. It had strong guidance.

You're seeing again a company who's going to benefit from the additional spending that's going into the economy because of the AI trade.

Rena Sherbill: We have a great episode from yesterday, from Thursday on our Investing Experts Podcast with Tech Contrarians. Some great takeaways on this recent earnings season in the tech space and also just on the major and the minor tech players right now, why there's been a software sell-off, et cetera. Really recommend that episode.

I wanted to highlight a stock that we were talking about last week, Hershey's (HSY). You were talking about how it's been doing so well for consecutive sessions. Somebody also pointed out the fact that it's Valentine's Day this weekend. Is it always in the run up to Valentine's Day? I know the seven or eight or nine consecutive sessions that it's been up, not necessarily, but is it always, is that a seasonal thing? I didn't even think about that, honestly.

Brian Stewart: Yeah, I haven't seen that. I feel like that's probably priced in a little bit. It seems it would be surprising to me.

I'm thinking of the Simpsons episode where somebody tells Homer to buy pumpkin futures ahead of Halloween. But then, he's in the black as Halloween comes up. He's like, no, I'm going to I'm going to keep it and it's going to keep going up. And then, it's like Homer surrounded by a bunch of rotting jack-o'-lanterns or whatever. So, yeah, I'd be surprised if that were true.

But that's an interesting question. I mean, that's something that someone should definitely look into.

Kind of on that same thought process, I was looking at DraftKings (DKNG) earlier today. I'm just sort of like a post Super Bowl kind of vibe. It's down 12% on its earnings. It had 43% revenue growth. Revenue was in line with expectations, but earnings, miss expectations and its monthly unique players was flat with last year.

So average revenue per player was up sharply, but the number of players that has is basically, I mean the fear among investors is that's plateauing. So you see a company who is having trouble bringing in new gamblers, but is able to get more revenue out of those gamblers.

I thought that was kind of a dark situation, like you have this cadre of gamblers, right? Where the company is just getting better and better at getting them to make bigger and bigger bets.

So I don't know if that's in the same realm as Hershey's, but I just thought post-Super Bowl that kind of thing is...

Rena Sherbill: But probably in the same realm as Homer getting into pumpkins, a la the Dutch into tulips. I mean, if you're watching the Super Bowl, it's like, should we all be gambling more and more and more? That's the vibe, how much can you possibly gamble in a recessionary, confusing, perplexing economy? It's very dystopian and dark, I would agree with you.

Brian Stewart: I think the Super Bowl ads were kind of interesting this year because there were a lot of AI ads, both ads produced by AI and for AI companies.

Rena Sherbill: Those Claude ones were great, I thought.

Brian Stewart: I was thinking about there was the Super Bowl where crypto was the big ad thing. Then you had the had some of those companies go under in a relatively short period of time after that happened.

And then I'm thinking back to Pets.com, kind of a famous Super Bowl ad that went sideways. So I think if you were AI bubble person, I think seeing all those Super Bowl ads would be, this is the top kind of thing. Doesn't mean it's true, but there is kind of a history of Super Bowl ads kind of calling the top of certain things.

Rena Sherbill: I smell a new index. I smell a new indicator. What else you got for us, Brian? We've got President's Day coming up. Anything about the Fed or would love to get into politics. Just kidding.

Brian Stewart: Yeah, unfortunately, talking about the Fed involves talking about politics. We have Warsh's nomination to confirmation. It's going to have to happen in a relatively near term. There's going to be some wrangling over that.

Fed minutes are coming out next week, so we're going to get a little bit more information about what the Fed was looking at when it made its last rate decision.

You also have economic data, another inflation marker. The PCE is coming out next week. So I think that's going to play into it a little bit. I don't expect any major surprises. The inflation data has been pretty steady. Unsurprisingly hotter than you'd want, but not upsettingly high and so I would think that that would keep going.

And then in terms of earnings, it was interesting. You were talking about the interview that you had and talking about sort of the smaller players in tech. And I think in terms of earnings, we've definitely gotten into more of a stock pickers situation.

I mean, there's a few more marquee like every week's going to have a couple of marquee names coming out, but there's a lot of smaller players that are announcing results. So I think investors should be sort of aware of some of those other opportunities, off the beaten path a little bit.

Next week in terms of the big names, Walmart's (WMT) the one to look for. Stock is trading at new highs. Recently topped a trillion dollars in market cap for the first time, first retailer to do that unless you count Amazon, which is more of tech company really.

And then you have a lot of worries about valuation for Walmart, but you also have Walmart in a pretty good spot in terms of if affordability is a concern, Walmart's in a good position to kind of capture that market. It also has the the size to in tariffs and other kind of cost situation, the situation where costs are concerning.

Walmart has the size to kind of squeeze suppliers and keep things cheap on that front. Also, it has the size. It's made deals lately. It's got to deal with Google. It's got to deal with OpenAI. Unclear how aggressively this is going to roll out to the functioning of the company. But it's a company that can take advantage of productivity increases and getting smarter about getting in front of consumers just because it has the resources to do that at a high scale.

So it'll be interesting to see what it has to say about both consumer spending habits and about the future of integrating some of these technological improvements into more mainstream activities.

Rena Sherbill: All right, off on Monday. Talk to you next week. Thanks, Brian.

Brian Stewart: Thank you, talk to you later.

data.title: Radcom: AI Traction, Strong Cash, And A Mispriced Stock (Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

RADCOM Ltd. (RDCM) is a company that provides cloud-based software to help telecom operators monitor, manage, and improve their increasingly complex 5G networks. In my last update, I changed my rating on the stock to a Buy. Profitability came in stronger, and recurring revenue kept climbing, which gave me more confidence in the model.

Overall, since my upgrade, the stock is marginally up. But this year, as you know, the shares have been headed in the wrong direction.

Nevertheless, after going through the latest earnings, I’m sticking with my bullish view. Management is still executing. Recurring revenue keeps building. And importantly, they’re still well positioned to benefit from rising demand for 5G and AI-driven network tools.

RADCOM Q4 Summary: What's Working

  • Q4 Revenue: $18.9M, +16% Y/Y.
  • FY 2025 Revenue: $71.5M, +17.2% Y/Y (above 15–18% guidance midpoint).

  • Q4 Non-GAAP EPS: $0.31 (beat by $0.05).
  • Q4 GAAP EPS: $0.21 vs. $0.14 Y/Y (+50%).
  • FY Non-GAAP EPS: $1.09 vs. $0.83 Y/Y (+31%).
  • FY GAAP EPS: $0.71 vs. $0.43 Y/Y (+65%).
  • Q4 Gross Margin: 77.6% (highest since 2018).
  • FY Gross Margin: 76.8% vs. 75.2% Y/Y.
  • FY Operating Income: $14.8M vs. $9.5M Y/Y (+55%).
  • FY Operating Margin: 20.6% vs. 15.6% Y/Y.
  • Q4 Operating Margin: 23% (8-year high).
  • FY Non-GAAP Net Income: $18.4M vs. $13.5M Y/Y.
  • Cash & Short-Term Deposits: $109.9M (record), no debt.
  • FY Cash Flow: $15.2M.
  • Q4 Cash Flow: $3.2M.

RADCOM's Bearish Signals

  • 2026 Revenue Guidance: 8%–12% growth (vs. 17.2% in 2025).
  • Q4 Sales & Marketing Growth: +1.4% Y/Y (slow relative to revenue growth).
  • FY R&D Expense Growth: +11.1% Y/Y ($18.5M).
  • Q4 R&D Expense Growth: +16.2% Y/Y ($4.9M).
  • High customer concentration (AT&T highlighted as a major driver).
  • Tier 1 sales cycle timing uncertainty (management: “hard to pinpoint”).
  • Capital allocation priority: pursuing M&A (execution risk implied).

My View On The Bull Case And The Friction Points

RADCOM's growth this quarter mostly came from expanding work with customers they already had. They also picked up a few new wins along the way. What’s driving it, though, is pretty straightforward. Operators are spending more on 5G optimization, and at the same time, they’re trying to layer in AI across their networks. So as they push in that direction, RADCOM is benefiting from that spend.

Management said their products are built to handle the growing need for real-time data analysis. Telecom companies are facing sharp increases in network traffic, especially from AI applications, which are putting pressure on older network systems. And this reflects a larger industry trend. In other words, as 5G networks continue to expand, telecom providers need better tools to monitor and understand their customers’ network experiences. At the same time, the number of IoT devices and overall data usage is increasing, adding even more strain on networks.

That said, customers are still acting cautiously. Big operators tend to drag their feet during technical evaluations. And when that happens, bookings get lumpy. Now, RADCOM does have some protection. Their high-capacity data capture tech is supposed to cut costs because it processes data more efficiently. That’s a real selling point.

Meanwhile, gross margins looked better this quarter. As you know, software scales. You don’t need to ramp hardware in the same way, so incremental revenue drops through at a higher margin. In addition, better day-to-day operations and stricter cost control allowed the company to complete more deployments while spending less per project, which also helped increase margins. Nevertheless, this efficiency isn’t exactly bulletproof. Semiconductor shortages from previous quarters continued into last year. And RADCOM depends on Nvidia’s (NVDA) DPUs as the core technology that powers its analytics systems. Not catastrophic, but definitely something to watch.

All things considered, the global shortage of computer chips may continue, although recent reports say the situation is improving as semiconductor factories increase production. It's something that I also discussed here when I last covered Entegris (ENTG). Prices are generally steady right now. Still, in established markets, basic network assurance tools have become more like standard, low-cost products. So this creates downward pressure on pricing and could limit RADCOM's growth potential if the company doesn't stand out by using advanced AI agents to differentiate its offerings.

In the plus column, the company’s cash flow became positive, helped by getting advance payments from multi-year contracts. This has created a strong cash reserve. Tier 1 customers might just view this large cash cushion as financial stability. Even so, it can also be a mixed blessing. Since the company has no debt, it may just be tempted to use the cash for M&A, something that came up on the call. When asked about the ~$200 million reserve, CEO Eppstein did, in fact, confirm that RADCOM's "first priority remains to look into M&A" and that it's a priority that management is actively looking to accelerate. But, just to play devil's advocate, if any acquired company doesn't fit well culturally or technologically, it could create integration problems and disrupt the company’s main flow of operations.

Beyond M&A, management basically sees this as a platform to innovate. For example, they’re leaning further into ServiceNow (NOW) to automate workflows. At the same time, telecom operators are moving toward sovereign cloud setups because of data rules. So, if data has to stay local, deployments get more complex. That could shape how RADCOM rolls out its product. And, as a result, they may need more capital tied up in compliant infrastructure.

That forward-looking stance is also reflected in the cost structure. Expenses went up because R&D stepped up. They’re spending more to build AI tools, especially telco-specific agents that can spot network issues and predict equipment failures. This lines up with what we’re seeing across the sector, something that I've been writing about a lot more frequently too since most companies are deploying these agent-style AI's just about everywhere.

Of course, higher R&D spending is only one piece of the operating equation. Sales and marketing spending edged up a bit. From what I can tell, they’re putting that money into nurturing the pipeline in higher-value regions. But G&A stayed flat. Management says this supports profitable growth. And sure, that makes sense on the surface. But if competition intensifies, take Ericsson (ERIC) and Huawei investing heavily in AI-driven RAN (Radio Access Network) technology, RADCOM may need to increase its own spending just to protect its market share. If that happens, the company’s profit margins could come under pressure.

One more final point here: mobility has been the sweet spot. That’s where they’re actually expanding. Big customers like AT&T (T) and Rakuten (RKUNY) use RADCOM technology to manage and monitor their virtual (software-based) networks instead of traditional hardware systems. At the same time, the company has become more efficient by focusing more on cloud-native solutions. That helps margins and makes the model more efficient, which is obviously a plus, too.

RDCM's Valuation

RDCM looks very attractively priced when you compare it to similar companies using the EV/Sales ratio. Right now, RADCOM stock is trading at 1.14x its TTM sales. In comparison, the average company in the same sector trades at 3.31x sales, about 65% lower than the median.

Factor in the forward EV/Sales ratio, and it drops even further to 1.05x expected future sales. The company's essentially priced as if it's barely growing, even though analysts expect EPS to increase by 7.8% in 2026 and 8.9% in 2027.

That $200 million also helps protect and cushion the company, so long as it does not overpay on any M&A or make the wrong strategic choice if revenue becomes uneven, especially if telecom operators delay testing or approving new projects, which can temporarily slow down bookings and sales.

There's still real competition out there, and that's never going to fade. This could squeeze profit margins, especially if R&D spending rises sharply in order to defend market share in AI-related tools. Even so, looking at valuation compared to the company’s own history, today’s EV/Sales multiple is 35% below its five-year average of 1.76x, hinting at strong reversion potential.

Final Takeaway

Strong Buy. Basically, RADCOM looks like it has more room to go up than down. If its valuation moves closer to what other companies in the same space are getting, the stock price could rise significantly. Plus, its growth is being supported by strong demand from 5G and AI. Throw in the ample cash reserve, and that gives it a solid financial cushion.

data.title: Transocean And Valaris Are Making A Great Move By Joining Forces
data.description:
The last few months now have been a really exciting time for shareholders of offshore drilling company Transocean (RIG). Since I reaffirmed the company as a “Hold” candidate back in December of last year, the stock has risen 44.6%. That dwarfs that 1.2% increase that the S&P 500 saw. The latest move that the company experienced came on Feb. 11, when shares rose 10.3%. This was followed, on Feb. 9, by news that the business is merging with another player in the space by the name of Valaris Ltd. (VAL) in an all-stock deal that will turn two sizable players in the industry into an industry giant.

In recent years, financial performance for both firms has been improving as industry conditions stabilize. Utilization rates are rising. And now that the worst is over for the space, it makes a lot of sense for some consolidation to occur.

Looking at the data provided, I view this transaction in a favorable light. There are certain trade-offs that investors should be aware of. For instance, Transocean is paying more for the cash flows it is receiving. But at the same time, the company is reducing its overall leverage situation. This will ultimately reduce the risk profile of the company, which is great considering how volatile this industry can be and how long downturns can last. Meanwhile, shareholders of Valaris are receiving a larger piece of the pie relative to the cash flow they bring to the table. Those investors will have to be more comfortable with greater net debt in relation to said cash flows. Personally, I think that these are appropriate trade-offs. And with the prospect of synergies, I would argue that rating both companies a soft “Buy” makes sense here.

A seismic move

On Feb. 19, the management team at Transocean issued a press release whereby they announced that the company and competitor Valaris will be combining into a single firm through an all-stock transaction initially valued at $5.8 billion. Technically, it is being advertised as an acquisition as opposed to a merger. But regardless of the terminology that you want to use, the valuation of $5.8 billion for Valaris, as well as the fact that it is a stock-oriented deal, will leave shareholders of that company owning 47% of the overall pie. The majority, 53%, will be controlled by investors in Transocean. This will be done by giving shareholders of Valaris 15.235 shares of Transocean for each share of Valaris they currently have.

The objective of this transaction is fairly straightforward. The companies are seeking to create an industry leader that provides diversified offshore services. The combined company will have a fleet of 73 rigs, with 33 of those being ultra-deepwater drillships, nine of them being semi-submersibles, and 31 being modern jackups. This is a compelling value proposition for those familiar with the space. This is in part because the assets of both companies operate in some of the most prolific and valuable offshore drilling regions in the world. This includes the Gulf of Mexico, Trinidad, Brazil, the Mediterranean, the North Sea, and elsewhere. All combined, about 78% of these semi-submersibles operate in harsh environments, while 35% of jackups do appear.

Over the last couple of years now, we have seen increased investments in offshore drilling. Back in 2024, for instance, about $190 billion of offshore upstream development capital was allocated to the space. In 2025, that was a bit higher at $193 billion. By the year 2027, overall spending is forecasted to climb to $209 billion. From 2025 through 2027, there should be a 156% surge in the number of projects sanctioned, with spending associated with these growing from $12 billion to $28 billion. Deepwater project sanctions are incredibly valuable because these are long-term projects that require significant spending by the companies operating the wells in question.

Already, the last few years have been a pretty good time for shareholders from a fundamental perspective. In the first chart above, you can see financial performance for Transocean covering the last three completed fiscal years and covering the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. The second chart above, meanwhile, shows the same thing when it comes to Valaris. Both companies have been beneficiaries of improving market conditions. In the chart below, as an example, you can see that the average daily rates for both companies have improved. While the utilization rate of rigs for Valaris has been pretty consistent, I am happy to see that Transocean has seen an increase over the last few years, with that rise coming due to robust demand in the deepwater space.

One of the beautiful things about this transaction is that it immediately creates greater backlog, which is essentially greater future revenue. At the end of the most recent quarter, Transocean boasted $6.73 billion worth of backlog. Meanwhile, Valaris had $4.45 billion worth. This figure for Valaris does exclude $2.16 billion of additional backlog that involves a joint venture that the company has a 50% ownership stake in. But for various reasons that go outside of the scope of this article, it's appropriate not to really focus on that. What we do know is that, as of the investor presentation regarding the transaction, the combined company is expected to have a backlog of about $10 billion. The numbers provided for each of the businesses in the transaction are a bit lower than what the most recent quarterly figures show. But that is very likely because of expected changes from one quarter to the next.

With additional scale should come the opportunity for significant cost savings. Previously, Transocean was already targeting $250 million in cost reductions for the 2025 through 2026 fiscal years. $100 million of that was captured in 2025, with the remaining $150 million expected to come in 2026. But in addition to this, management expects an additional $200 million in annual run rate synergies. This will come from consolidating overlapping global operations and shore base support, streamlining operations, exercising greater supply chain savings from supply providers, eliminating redundant general and administrative costs, and more.

Using guidance provided by the management teams at both enterprises, as well as historical results achieved by both firms for the 2024 fiscal years, you can see in the table above pro forma operating cash flow and EBITDA for both companies individually as well as on a combined basis. This gives us a firm understanding of what the two companies should look like when they complete their transaction. Using these estimates, you can also see how, in the chart below, I valued the two firms individually, and I also valued them on a combined basis with and without synergies.

What's fascinating about this transaction is that while investors in Valaris are very clearly being paid a hefty premium, if synergies do come to fruition, then even Transocean might see its overall trading multiple drop on at least an EV-to-EBITDA basis. This means that if everything goes according to plan, then there's the opportunity for shareholders of both companies, from a valuation standpoint, to win. To be clear, the picture could look even better than this thanks to the projected $150 million in cost savings that Transocean is aiming to capture this year. I did not include that, however, for the sake of conservatism.

If we focus solely on valuation, investors in Valaris are walking away from this transaction with the best deal. That company is bringing only 36.5% of operating cash flow and 35.3% of EBITDA to the combined business. And yet, shareholders of that enterprise get 47% of equity in it. Where the tradeoff comes from is the difference in net debt that both companies have. I say this because Transocean is bringing $5.39 billion of net debt into the picture. By comparison, net debt for Valaris is only about $410 million.

In the chart above, you can see the net leverage ratio of both companies on their own, as well as on a combined basis. Even if synergies are not captured, we are looking at a more moderate net leverage ratio for Transocean, which makes sense why the company is getting a disproportionately small share of the cash flows that the combined enterprise will generate. As the aforementioned chart also shows, leverage for the combined business will still be above what other players in the space have. However, management said that, if all goes according to plan, the net leverage ratio of the company should contract to about 1.5 within 24 months of the closing of the deal.

Takeaway

Based on the totality of the circumstances, I must say that I am impressed with this maneuver. There are certain trade-offs that investors in both companies will have to be comfortable with. But I don't really see a winner here versus a loser. Both companies win in certain ways while giving up something in return. If we talk solely about valuation, then Valaris is very obviously the winner here. But if everything goes perfectly, even Transocean can score a small win on that front. Leverage needs to be factored in here, though the overall trajectory there looks positive, and with the promise that it will fall further, investors have multiple reasons to be optimistic about this development. At the end of the day, I think that this is enough to justify upgrading Transocean to a soft “Buy” while also assigning a soft “Buy” to Valaris.

data.title: Pinterest: The Market Has Lost Its Mind, 25% Net Cash, Big Buybacks
data.description:
Pinterest, Inc. (PINS) cratered following its fourth quarter earnings release. The company’s results were not inspiring, as they featured decelerating growth even as its larger peer is going in the opposite direction. While the outlook looks muddy, the stock is too cheap here. The company’s net cash balance sheet and robust GAAP margins are underrepresented with the stock trading at under 10x earnings. The company is aggressively repurchasing shares, and I expect this to eventually make an impact. I reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

PINS Stock Price

I last covered PINS in January, where I rated the stock a Strong Buy following their layoff announcement. The stock has cratered 35% since.

There is irony in that the stock appears to be falling for the already-present risks that I have been acknowledging in my reports regarding relevance. The market appears to not care about valuation, but I do not expect that ambivalence to last long as the share repurchase machine is still kicking in full gear.

PINS Stock Key Metrics

PINS is a social media company known for allowing users to put “pins” into ideas, often to organize project plans. In the most recent quarter, the company generated 14% YoY revenue growth to $1.319 billion, coming in at the low end of guidance of between $1.313 billion and $1.338 billion. For a company that typically outperforms the high end of guidance, I suspect that many investors viewed this result as “missing guidance.”

Judging from the stock decline, one would expect this to come from slowing user growth, but this was not the case. Monthly active users (‘MAUs’) jumped 12% YoY, including solid growth in the U.S. & Canada.

Instead, the issue came from average revenue per user, which grew by only 2% YoY. The market may be viewing this as a red flag as the company’s ARPU is already significantly lower than Meta Platforms (META) by a wide margin.

The company also adjusted EBITDA margin stall at 41%.

The company was also profitable on a GAAP basis, but also did not see meaningful margin improvement in the quarter. Speaking bluntly, there simply was not a lot to like about this quarter.

PINS ended the quarter with $2.5 billion of cash versus no debt. The company repurchased $500 million of stock in the quarter, up from $100 million in the prior year. With the stock continuing to struggle, I expect management to lean even further into the share repurchase program.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see between 11% and 14% YoY revenue growth. Given that META continues to post accelerating growth (at a far larger size), the implied deceleration in growth rates has not been met warmly by investors. The adjusted EBITDA margin target of 19% may have also been viewed with disappointment by investors.

On the conference call, management noted that they had “absorbed an exogenous shock this year related to tariffs,” with that impact “disproportionately affecting ad spend from our top retail advertisers.” To counteract this, management stated that they were “doubling down” on increasing their exposure to SMBs but that it “may take a couple of quarters to play out.”

I suspect that investors were not satisfied with this response, as there’s an elephant in the room: if tariffs were to blame, then why didn’t META see the same weakness? It looks like even though PINS and META are two distinctly different platforms with distinctly different use cases, META’s strong execution may be stealing advertising budgets.

Is PINS Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, PINS traded at just 8.6x 2026e earnings.

Based on estimates for around 13% forward growth, PINS stock traded at just 2.05x 2025e sales.

Net cash now makes up 25% of the market cap. Given that the company is solidly profitable on a GAAP basis, it does make sense to exclude that net cash from the valuation. Net of cash, the stock trades at just 1.53x forward sales. Assuming the company reaches 30% net margins over the long term, the stock is trading at just 5.1x long-term earnings. I note that with the company already generating a 22% GAAP operating margin, that 30% net margin assumption looks arguably conservative.

I have an unfortunate tendency of occasionally buying stocks based on valuation even if the story is ugly. This appears to be no exception, but I’ll stubbornly defend my case. Sure, there are plenty of signs indicating that PINS is losing to META. The past investment thesis centered around “smaller size means a longer growth runway” is more or less moot at this point.

But valuation absolutely does matter here for three crucial reasons. First, the company maintains a strong net cash balance sheet with no debt—value traps often carry significant debt positions. Second, the company is solidly GAAP profitable, and operating leverage is likely. This is the kind of business that does not inherently require increased OpEx to drive revenue growth. While margins are not expanding in the near term, my point is that this is not necessarily an inherent problem. Finally, this is a management team that has already shown a willingness and commitment to share repurchases. With stocks that trade cheaply (while possessing a broken story), share repurchases are the most important catalyst to drive a turnaround. I am very doubtful the stock will stay down so low simply due to the upward pressure from share repurchases that might be able to repurchase as much as 10% of shares this year alone (based on just 20% margins). I expect the stock to re-rate to at least 12x to 15x earnings, implying around 50% potential upside.

PINS Stock Risks

It is possible that the platform is being disrupted (mainly by META). In this scenario, growth might decelerate meaningfully or even turn flat. This might impede the company’s ability to post operating leverage (but I again emphasize the importance of the company already generating a 22% GAAP operating margin). It is possible that management engages in expensive M&A, which dilutes shareholders. It is possible that the economy turns into a recession, which negatively impacts advertising spend.

PINS Stock Conclusion

PINS legitimately might be losing to META. The company is significantly smaller yet struggling to post anywhere close to the same growth rates. That said, the company’s net cash balance sheet and high GAAP profit margins should not be ignored, nor should the company’s committed share repurchase program. META is a top pick in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio, but I cannot omit the deep undervaluation of PINS. I reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Walmart: Great Execution, Terrible Entry Point (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Walmart Inc. (WMT) is making an aggressive push into AI, rolling out tools like its assistant Sparky to squeeze more efficiency out of a sprawling operation that runs on thin margins. It's already helping the company work more efficiently. Last quarter, Walmart’s operating income increased along with sales growth, and its e-commerce business became profitable. With these improvements, Walmart is putting itself in a stronger position to compete with Amazon in retail.

Be that as it may, I am taking a very bearish view. The market is pricing this stock as if it has already executed a flawless AI transformation and successfully fended off Amazon. That’s unrealistic. There are real risks here. Integrating AI is difficult and expensive, and Amazon is relentless. None of that appears to be reflected in the current price.

The Spark Powering Walmart Inc.'s Retail Reinvention

As February 19 approaches and Walmart prepares to report Q4 earnings, I think there’s a bigger story here. Specifically, who controls the next phase of retail profitability in North America? And right now, Walmart is making a serious run at territory long dominated by Amazon (AMZN). Not with price cuts. Not with store openings. But with agents. AI agents.

Last quarter, Walmart increased its operating income by 8% even though sales only grew by 5.9%. That shows the company is becoming more efficient and making more profit from each dollar of sales. And if Walmart’s Q4 results match Wall Street expectations, EPS of $0.72 and revenue of $188.46 billion, it would confirm that this improvement is continuing. It would also mean Walmart is closing the profitability gap with Amazon, whose North American retail business reported a 9% operating margin in its most recent quarter.

One example of how it intends to get there is via Sparky, Walmart’s generative AI shopping assistant. On one level, it's kind of like a simple shopping alarm clock. For example, if a family buys the same items every week, Sparky picks up on that. Then it reminds them to reorder those staples automatically. But it also builds smart bundles. Basically, it pairs everyday essentials with related add-ons customers might want. So if you’re buying the usual basics, it suggests complementary products at the same time.

Okay, so if you're an avid Amazon user like I am, this all probably seems vaguely familiar and not that all revolutionary. I can’t remember the last time Amazon didn’t suggest similar or related products when I was about to buy something. It’s always showing items that match or goes well with what I’m looking at. And honestly, for Walmart, if that similar strategy works, then it makes sense to improve it even more, which is exactly the point I’m trying to make.

In Walmart's case, it's all about the upsell opportunities. When I first got into the retail side of finance, I always remembered my manager's sales mantra: "Would you like extra fries with that?" Although that was more about offering an annuity or mutual fund, back when those were more en vogue.

That mindset hasn’t disappeared; it’s just evolved. Sparky is built into Walmart’s four super agents framework. This means that customer-facing tools like Sparky are connected to Walmart’s behind-the-scenes systems. Together, they help optimize everything from inventory forecasting to associate workflows.

In supply chains, AI agents help predict customer demand and change delivery routes in real time. This reduces waste and cuts down on costly mistakes that used to hurt profit margins. Meanwhile, merchant dashboards chew through mountains of data and spit out recommendations so teams can push out new products without waiting on a room full of analysts to catch up. For example, Walmart’s Trend-to-Product system shortens fashion production cycles by up to 18 weeks. This allows Walmart to spot trends earlier, turn them into products more quickly, and sell them with less leftover inventory.

And that’s where the flywheel begins to spin faster. A recent survey shows that 81% of Walmart shoppers use Sparky to check product details and see if items are in stock before making a purchase. This high level of use shows strong customer engagement, which helps increase the chances that shoppers actually complete their purchases. At the same time, the company’s retail media division grew by 33% compared to the previous year. This growth was 6x faster than the company’s overall revenue growth. It now accounts for about one-third of Walmart’s operating income. A large portion of that growth is boosted by sponsored prompts that are built directly into Sparky’s conversations. Once you wire the place for execution, it spreads. On store floors, AI tells associates what to do first. Online, the goal is to move e-commerce from 19% of revenue last fiscal year to 50% within five years.

As it stands, over 50% of Walmart's e-commerce fulfillment volume now runs through automated centers, cutting shipping costs by double digits for seven consecutive quarters. Last quarter, CEO McMillon also indicated that, thanks to AI agents, they enabled same-day delivery for 35% of digital orders. It's only going to improve from here.

All of this mirrors Amazon's robotics push, deployed with Walmart's scale. It's a machine swarm of fulfillment, yielding 40% better unit productivity. That stands in contrast to Amazon’s $200 billion capex splash on AI infrastructure, something I recently covered when upgrading Amazon to a Strong Buy. But that's internal spend for Amazon. In parallel, Walmart’s strategy focuses heavily on partnerships with Google Gemini and OpenAI. By working closely with these companies, Walmart reduces how much it needs to spend on building everything itself, while still benefiting from the growth. At least on the surface, the strategy appears to be delivering. Last quarter, Walmart’s e-commerce sales increased by 27%. Even more importantly, its e-commerce business became profitable for the first time. This is a major shift from previous quarters, when online operations were losing money and putting pressure on overall profit margins.

In all fairness, Profitero’s 2025 U.S. Price Wars study showed that Amazon still had the lowest online prices among major U.S. retailers, which is why it's still my first go-to when shopping online, with prices about 14% lower on average than other large online competitors. While Walmart was closer than most other competitors to Amazon in price, it is narrowing that gap to about 4% higher on average than Amazon’s price. Better, yes, just not good enough.

Looking ahead, management thinks that agentic commerce and AI-powered personalization will help drive long-term growth, rather than immediately boosting profit margins. For the full year, operating income is expected to grow between 4.8% and 5.5%. As for Q4, the company expects stronger growth of 8% to 11%. Of course, there's always risks. If Walmart’s supply chain AI struggles during periods of unpredictable demand, it'll hurt performance. Also, relying too heavily on partnerships could limit how much control Walmart has over its own data. That might weaken its ability to compete with Amazon and its $142 billion AWS juggernaut.

A Richly Valued Walmart

Right now, WMT’s blended P/E ratio is currently 50.44x, according to FAST Graphs. Just on optics, if I didn't know this chart represented Walmart, I would've guessed some kind of small or mid-cap meme tech stock. That said, WMT's historical average P/E is 19.49x, well above a conservative fair value level of 15x.

Looking at the forecasts, its estimated fair value's way below, around $40. Needless to say, it's overvalued on these measures alone. Meanwhile, expected earnings growth is projected at just 9.84% per year, which is relatively modest. So, in theory, if the P/E ratio falls back toward its historical average, investors could face an estimated return of -25.56%.

Looking at another measure, Walmart’s EV/EBITDA ratio is 26.44x. That too is much higher than the sector median of 11.88x, a 122% premium. For me, this raises a real question about whether the stock has run too far, too fast, and whether it can actually hold up from here. Especially if the macro backdrop gets tougher. As for a deeper dive on that topic, check out SA analyst's Jack Bowman's take on how "The Consumer Is Tapping Out."

If there were a margin of safety hiding somewhere else in the financials, I'd hope to expect to find it in cash flow. But that’s not the case either. Cash flow growth projections suggest EPS could grow between 5% and 12% annually through 2029. However, Walmart’s price-to-cash flow ratio is 25.99x, compared to the sector’s 11.64x. That's another large premium to contend with. And with an extremely low dividend yield, it doesn't give you much of a cushion either.

Final Verdict

Strong sell. Normally, Walmart’s convenience, ubiquitous footprint, massive scale, and long-standing household brand would justify me giving it some extra flexibility in a final rating. But the current valuation numbers are simply too extreme, even absurd. And while Walmart’s AI-driven margin improvements do show great promise, the stock is trading at 50x blended earnings, roughly 3x its historical average. At this level, the price already bakes in very optimistic expectations heading into earnings next week.

data.title: Belden Q4: The Supercycle Is Going To Lift Everyone, But Be Cautious
data.description:
Introduction

Belden Inc. (BDC) just reported Q4 and full-year numbers, so I thought I’d go through them in more detail and give some comments on its outlook and why I think we will see further upside ahead.

By the Numbers

Looking at the headline numbers, sales for Q4 came in at $720m, up around 8% y/y, beating analysts’ estimates by around $23m. The breakdown of revenue segments shows us that revenues increased 5% organically, with Automation Solutions up 10% to $2.715B, while Smart Infrastructure came in flat. For the full year, revenues were up 10% and 6% organically, with AS leading the charge with 11% growth, while SIS was up 1%.

So, the clear revenue driver is the Automation solutions, while SIS lagged quite a bit and dragged the company’s overall performance. The automation surge can be attributed to some tailwinds in efficiency, higher solutions penetration, and decent vertical strength. The management actually said during the call that high inflation and labor shortages benefited the company. Also, solutions-based wins accounted for over 20% of the segment’s revenue, with strong demand in the energy, discrete manufacturing, and mass transit sectors also playing a role. The weak broadband and telco sector didn’t help the SIS segment, as inventory overhang, the transition between technologies from older DOCSIS standards to newer fiber, and the slump in commercial real estate played the main roles in such a sluggish performance overall.

Moving on to the company’s profitability and efficiency, Q4 non-GAAP EPS came in at around $2.08, beating estimates by 13 cents, so it was a decent beat, and is up 23% y/y. A record for the company. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $459m, an increase of 12%, with an adjusted EBITDA margin coming in at 16.9%, up 20bps compared to the prior year. Adjusted EPS for the year came in at $7.54, up 19%. So, we can see that the company is quite a bit more profitable this time around. The shift in mix towards the higher-margin solutions, like the integrated packages that come with hardware and software, helped the company achieve higher profitability.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. BDC ended the year with around $390m in cash and equivalents and almost $1.3B in long-term debt. How worrisome is this? For the full year, the company’s interest expense was around $46m, while GAAP operating income was around $315m, so its interest coverage ratio is around 7x, which is quite healthy and well above analysts’ requirements of at least 2x. Additionally, the company’s operating cash flow was around $355m, and free cash flow was around $219m. With over $390m in cash and good cash flow performance, this debt is not an issue.

The management was kind enough to provide us with some color on the next quarter’s performance. Sales at the midpoint should be around $682.5m, implying around 9.2% y/y growth, which came in above consensus estimates of around $661.2m. Adjusted EPS should be in the range of $1.65-$1.75, above consensus expectations of $1.68, if we take the midpoint to be $1.70.

We can see that investors liked the double beat and the surprisingly strong guidance for the next quarter. The company’s share price reached new all-time highs on the day, going up as much as to reach $159.99, but due to market jitters since then, the company’s share price has come down slightly to around $150, which is still a good performance despite the wobbly market.

I liked the report. It showed us that the company is benefiting quite a bit from the high-demand end-markets like data centers, and the guidance showed us that the momentum is accelerating.

Comments on the Outlook

During the conference call, we could see where the management is looking to take the company going forward, and that is to follow the money. Enterprises are looking to spend a vast amount of dollars on upgrading their infrastructure, and BDC is looking to capitalize on this significantly by pivoting and focusing on the only growth story of the past few years that is somewhat exciting, the AI growth story and data centers. The CEO, Ashish Chand, mentioned that the AI data center pipeline more than doubled in 2025, which was still quite a small part of the total revenues; it is being looked at as one of the major revenue drivers for 2026, along with the BEAD program, which is a government-funded broadband expansion. The company expanded its AI data center by 300% as they see a lot of growth opportunity here, and BEAD funding will certainly flow in this year.

To support this pivot into being a complete solutions provider, the management decided that it would be best to restructure the company’s revenue segments and to eliminate them, as they believe that Smart Infrastructure was hiding high-growth areas like data centers, as the segment was grouped with slow-moving broadband. The goal now is to move to a “Unified Functional Model.” I’m still not sure how that is going to look. I’m very curious to see how the company is going to show this change in the next quarter’s report.

Clearly, like with many companies associated in some way with AI data centers, BDC is chasing the money, and that is fine with me. It looks like this is the only way to see any sort of improvements in profitability and top-line growth. We are witnessing right now, probably, one of the most lopsided economic expansions in tech history. AI infrastructure is not only growing faster than any other business segment, but it is currently the only segment that is in a super cycle, with many other legacy industries either stagnant or having some low single-digit growth. Analysts are seeing that companies aren’t just spending more money on data centers and AI infrastructure, but they are seeing that they are redirecting money away from general-purpose servers and legacy systems to fund AI clusters, calling it a Zero-Sum game. This cannibalization of capital is unprecedented, so obviously, you have to go where the money is. That means I can see the company’s share price continue to rise for much longer, unless the growth expected doesn’t materialize, in which case, the company’s share price will come back down to earth relatively quickly.

Should you invest now?

I think it is important to check the company’s intrinsic value to see what would be a good share price to start a position, given the promising growth outlook on the back of data center and AI growth.

Below are the assumptions I used.

For revenues, I am modelling that due to data center demand, the company will see a boost; however, it won’t be a massive boost since the data center revenues are still low compared to the rest of the revenues the company generates. The same for operating margins; I can see further profitability improvements because AI data center infrastructure demands higher margins, so the company’s profitability will improve. For the rest of the assumptions, I went with the company’s historical averages and a tax rate that is more aligned with a company that essentially doesn’t have any more tax credits and will be paying the full corporate tax rate in the U.S.

With the assumptions, we get the following EPS projections.

These are slightly above what analysts are expecting the company to do over the next few years, but I’m satisfied with these projections.

With that said, the company’s intrinsic value is around $150 a share, which means the company is trading at its fair value.

If you are looking for more exposure to the only growing business segment I mentioned, then I would say it is not a bad time to buy some shares in BDC. However, for me, I am not looking to add more concentration risk at the moment, as my portfolio continues to be overweighted in AI and data center growth stories. So, I am going to assign the company a Hold rating.

If you are happy with owning the company as it has already gone up over the last few years, there is no need to sell yet, as there will be more tailwinds ahead. Trimming a bit off the top is advisable, but leaving most of the position is probably a good idea. I’ve been trimming my best performer, but they keep climbing up, so they continue to be massive, and I believe that is going to go on for at least another couple of years, given how hyperscalers are looking to spend billions of dollars more on their AI dreams.
